cases.title: Weapon Visible on Approach
cases.presentation: EMS is dispatched for a 'person acting erratically.' On approach, the crew observes the patient pacing on the porch, shouting, and appears to be holding a knife.

vitals.scene: Weapon visible, patient agitated and shouting

cases.question: What is the correct first action?
options: A: Approach immediately to begin a medical assessment.
B: Stage at a safe distance and involve law enforcement per local protocol before any approach.
C: Call out to the patient to drop the weapon and walk toward them.
D: Attempt de-escalation techniques while standing close to the patient.
cases.explanation: A visible weapon and an agitated, shouting patient represent a credible scene-safety concern. The correct response is to stage at a safe distance and involve law enforcement per local protocol before any provider approaches — provider safety must be established before patient contact can safely occur.
cases.clinicalPearl: A visible weapon changes everything — stage and call for law enforcement before you get close, no matter how urgent the call sounds.

cases.title: SEA-3 in Practice
cases.presentation: EMS is called to check on a 45-year-old man reported as 'not himself.' On scene, he is disheveled, has not bathed in what appears to be days, paces continuously, and speaks in a rapid, tangential, difficult-to-follow manner.
cases.question: How should this presentation be organized using the SEA-3 framework?
options: A: These findings are unrelated and should be assessed independently with no combined significance.

B: Appearance (disheveled/poor hygiene), Activity (pacing), and Speech (rapid, tangential) together suggest a higher-risk overall picture than any single finding alone, warranting careful, calm assessment.
C: This presentation requires no further behavioral assessment since no weapon is present.
D: This is simply a medical complaint requiring no behavioral framework.
cases.explanation: Using SEA-3, the poor grooming/hygiene (Appearance), continuous pacing (Activity), and rapid tangential speech (Speech) together paint a more complete and concerning clinical picture than any single finding alone, guiding a careful, calm, structured assessment.
cases.clinicalPearl: SEA-3 findings compound — several concerning findings together tell you more than any one finding does alone.

cases.title: Respecting a Competent Refusal
cases.presentation: A 38-year-old woman called EMS during a panic attack that has since resolved. She is alert, oriented, and coherent, and after discussing risks and benefits, clearly and repeatedly declines transport to the hospital.

vitals.decision: Repeated, clear refusal after risk/benefit discussion

cases.question: What is the correct approach?
options: A: Physically restrain and transport her against her wishes.
B: Document the informed refusal per protocol, provide appropriate resources/follow-up guidance, and respect her autonomy as a competent patient.
C: Leave without any documentation or resource information, considering the call complete.
D: Continue to ask the same questions repeatedly until she agrees to transport.
cases.explanation: This patient demonstrates capacity (alert, oriented, coherent) and has made a clear, informed decision to refuse transport. The correct approach is to document the informed refusal per protocol and provide appropriate resources — respecting her autonomy. Leaving without documentation, however, would risk being interpreted as inadequate care, distinct from abandonment (which involves an incompetent or higher-need patient losing continuity of care).
cases.clinicalPearl: A competent, informed refusal isn't abandonment — but skipping the documentation and resource-sharing step is still bad practice.
